active_paradigms.idea: Greedy maximum-radius placement with numpy grid search
active_paradigms.description: Place circles one at a time using greedy maximization. For each new circle, search over a fine grid of candidate positions (e.g., 50x50 grid). At each candidate position, compute maximum possible radius as min(distance to all walls, distance to each existing circle minus that circle's radius). Use numpy broadcasting to compute all distances efficiently: compute distances to all existing circles simultaneously, subtract their radii, take minimum. Track the position yielding largest radius and place the circle there. This naturally places larger circles in corners first, then edges, then interior.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii by maximizing each circle's radius during placement
active_paradigms.cautions: Grid resolution affects quality - use at least 50x50. Must handle case where no valid position exists (shouldn't happen for 26 circles). Use numpy broadcasting for efficiency: distances = np.sqrt(np.sum((candidate_positions[:, None, :] - existing_centers[None, :, :])**2, axis=2))

active_paradigms.idea: Contact chain construction with analytical solution
active_paradigms.description: Model the packing as a system of contact equations. Design a contact graph where: 4 corner circles each touch 2 walls, 8 edge circles touch 1 wall, and interior circles touch neighbors. Set up equations: for wall contacts (x=r, x=1-r, y=r, y=1-r), for circle contacts ((x1-x2)²+(y1-y2)²=(r1+r2)²). Use numpy.linalg.lstsq to solve the overdetermined system. Start with a hexagonal-inspired contact pattern but allow variable radii. The key is that circles touching 2 walls can have r up to 1/(2+√2) ≈ 0.293, much larger than interior circles.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii by solving for optimal radii that satisfy contact constraints
active_paradigms.cautions: System may be over/under-determined - use least squares. Need to validate solution doesn't violate constraints. May need iterative refinement if initial solution is infeasible. Parameterize using relative positions along contact chains.

active_paradigms.idea: Hierarchical corner-edge-interior packing with variable radii
active_paradigms.description: Use a three-phase hierarchical approach: Phase 1 places 4 large corner circles (each can have radius up to 0.293 by touching two walls). Phase 2 places ~8 edge circles between corners (touching one wall). Phase 3 fills interior with remaining circles. For each phase, compute maximum radius using numpy.minimum of wall distances and distances to already-placed circles. This exploits the geometric insight that corner circles can be ~3x larger than typical interior circles, dramatically increasing total sum.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii by prioritizing large circles in high-value positions (corners, then edges)
active_paradigms.cautions: Corner circle radius = min(x, y, 1-x, 1-y) where position is chosen to maximize this. For a corner at (r, r), radius can be r if it only touches two walls. Must ensure corner circles don't overlap with each other. Use numpy.minimum to compute constraints from all directions simultaneously.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.differential_evolution for global optimization with penalty method
tried_paradigms.description: Apply scipy.optimize.differential_evolution to globally optimize all 78 variables. Since differential_evolution doesn't directly support constraints, use a penalty method: add large penalty terms to the objective for constraint violations (circles outside square, overlapping circles). The objective becomes: -sum(radii) + penalty_weight * (boundary_violations + overlap_violations). Use population_size=15-20, mutation=(0.5, 1), recombination=0.7, and polish=True for local refinement. This population-based approach can escape local minima.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Primary score with global search capability
tried_paradigms.cautions: Choose appropriate penalty weight (start with 100-1000); differential_evolution is slower but finds better global solutions; use bounds for all variables (radii in [0, 0.5], positions in [0, 1]); set maxiter appropriately for 600s timeout

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.linprog for optimal radius computation given fixed positions
tried_paradigms.description: The current code uses proportional scaling when circles overlap, which is suboptimal. For FIXED positions, the radius optimization is actually a LINEAR program: maximize sum(r_i) subject to r_i + r_j <= dist(i,j) for all pairs, and r_i <= dist_to_wall(i) for boundary constraints. Use scipy.optimize.linprog with method='highs' to solve this exactly. The objective vector c = -ones(n) (negative because linprog minimizes), constraint matrix A_ub encodes pairwise non-overlap and boundary constraints, and bounds ensure non-negative radii. This finds the globally optimal radii for any given positions.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii given fixed positions
tried_paradigms.cautions: Ensure constraint matrix is properly constructed. Use method='highs' for robustness. The positions must be reasonable - linprog only optimizes radii, not positions. Consider using better initial positions from hexagonal patterns.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi to refine circle positions iteratively
tried_paradigms.description: In optimal circle packing, each circle's position relates to its Voronoi cell. Compute the Voronoi diagram of current positions using scipy.spatial.Voronoi. For each circle, move it toward the centroid of its Voronoi cell (clipped to unit square). The Voronoi cell boundaries naturally define the 'territory' each circle can occupy. After position refinement, compute radii as the minimum distance to cell boundaries and square edges. This geometric approach avoids optimization entirely and uses the natural structure of the packing problem.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: circle positions through Voronoi cell centroids
tried_paradigms.cautions: Handle infinite Voronoi cells at boundaries by clipping. Add mirror points outside the unit square to create finite boundary cells. Iterate until positions stabilize (typically 10-20 iterations). The Voronoi approach works best when combined with good initial positions.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Delaunay to identify neighbors for efficient radius computation
tried_paradigms.description: The current O(n²) pairwise distance computation is inefficient and the proportional scaling is suboptimal. Use scipy.spatial.Delaunay to compute the Delaunay triangulation of circle centers. Only circles that share a Delaunay edge can potentially overlap, reducing constraint checks from O(n²) to O(n). For each circle, compute its radius as the minimum of: distance to walls, and for each Delaunay neighbor, (distance - neighbor's radius). Process circles in order of decreasing 'freedom' (corners first, then edges, then interior) to prioritize larger circles.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: radius computation efficiency and optimality through neighbor-aware constraints
tried_paradigms.cautions: Delaunay triangulation may miss some overlapping pairs near boundaries. Verify all pairwise constraints after Delaunay-based computation. The priority ordering (corners > edges > interior) is crucial - corners have more freedom and should be assigned larger radii first.

tried_paradigms.description: Implement a simulation where circles start at minimum size and iteratively expand. Each iteration: 1) Try to grow each circle by a small amount, 2) Check for collisions with walls and other circles, 3) If collision would occur, limit growth. Use numpy for distance calculations. Process circles in random or priority order. Continue until no circle can grow further. This naturally finds a local maximum without scipy.optimize.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii by allowing circles to expand to their natural maximum
tried_paradigms.cautions: Must handle the order of expansion carefully - larger circles should perhaps be prioritized. Use sufficient iterations for convergence. Start with good initial positions (corners first, then edges, then hexagonal interior).

tried_paradigms.idea: Optimized geometric construction with greedy radius assignment
tried_paradigms.description: Design an explicit placement strategy based on geometric analysis: 1) Place 4 corner circles at optimal positions (touching two walls each, radius ≈ 0.15-0.17), 2) Place edge circles in gaps between corners, 3) Fill interior with hexagonal pattern. Then compute radii greedily: for each circle i, set r_i = min(distance to walls, distance to all other circles / 2). Process in order of decreasing potential radius. This avoids the proportional scaling problem in the current code.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii through better initial placement and greedy radius computation
tried_paradigms.cautions: The order of radius computation matters - compute larger circles first. Ensure positions are carefully chosen to maximize space utilization. May need to adjust positions iteratively after seeing computed radii.

tried_paradigms.idea: Largest-first packing with contact chain optimization
tried_paradigms.description: Build the packing by placing circles one at a time, always placing the largest possible circle next. Algorithm: 1) Start with 4 corner circles at maximum possible size (each can have r ≈ 0.146 if they don't touch each other), 2) Find the largest gap and place a circle there, 3) Repeat until 26 circles placed. For each new circle, compute its maximum radius as the minimum of: distance to each wall, distance to each existing circle minus that circle's radius. This ensures each new circle touches at least one constraint.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii by prioritizing larger circles and ensuring tight packing
tried_paradigms.cautions: Need efficient gap-finding algorithm. Use candidate positions at midpoints between existing circles and walls. Verify final solution meets all constraints. The greedy approach may not find global optimum but should significantly improve over current.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use explicit hand-tuned coordinates from known optimal packings with direct radius calculation via linear system
tried_paradigms.description: Research the known optimal packing for n=26 circles. Place circles explicitly using coordinates derived from published optimal packings or mathematical analysis. Instead of iterative radius reduction, compute radii directly: for each circle, radius = min(distance to each wall, distance to each other circle / 2). This gives each circle its maximum possible radius given the constraints. The key insight is that the current program uses a suboptimal 5-4-5-4-5-3 pattern - better patterns exist like 4-5-4-5-4-4 or asymmetric arrangements that better utilize corner space.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii by using optimal spatial arrangement
tried_paradigms.cautions: Must ensure no overlaps in the final configuration. The radius calculation must account for ALL constraints simultaneously, not just nearest neighbor. Use a greedy approach: sort circles by limiting constraint and assign radii accordingly.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use corner-heavy asymmetric packing with large circles in corners and smaller circles filling gaps
tried_paradigms.description: Place 4 large circles in corners (each can have radius up to 0.5 if alone). Then fill edges with medium circles, and pack the center with smaller circles. The key insight: corners are the most valuable real estate because a circle in a corner touches 2 walls, limiting its position but allowing large radius. Design: 4 corner circles (~0.15-0.2 each), 8 edge circles along each side, 14 interior circles in hexagonal pattern. Compute radii using the direct formula: r_i = min(wall distances, (dist_ij - r_j) for all j < i).
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii by prioritizing high-value corner positions
tried_paradigms.cautions: Corner circles must be placed carefully - too large and they block edge placement. Use asymmetric positioning: corner circles slightly offset from exact corners to allow edge circles to fit. The radius assignment must be sequential, respecting previously assigned radii.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.distance.cdist for efficient constraint computation with greedy sequential placement
tried_paradigms.description: Start with empty square. Place circles one by one using greedy strategy: for each new circle, find the position that maximizes its possible radius given already-placed circles. Use scipy.spatial.distance.cdist to efficiently compute distances to all existing circles. For each candidate position (on a grid or along walls), compute max radius = min(dist to walls, min(dist to each existing circle - its radius)). Place circle at position with largest possible radius. This constructs a near-optimal packing without iterative optimization.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum_radii by greedy maximization at each step
tried_paradigms.cautions: Grid resolution matters - too coarse misses good positions, too fine is slow. Use adaptive refinement: coarse grid first, then refine around best positions. Consider placing larger circles first (corners, then edges, then interior) for better overall packing.
